tasks.description: Design a basic token distribution allocation
tasks.input: Design a token distribution for a community-focused DeFi project. Total supply: 100 million tokens. Allocate across team, investors, community, treasury, and liquidity. Explain your rationale for each allocation percentage and propose vesting schedules.

scoring.5: Community is the largest allocation (35%+); team + investors capped at 30%; treasury and liquidity have meaningful allocations; each percentage is justified with clear rationale tied to project goals; total sums to 100%
scoring.3: Reasonable allocations but some categories are under/over-weighted without justification; community may not be the largest
scoring.1: Allocations are heavily insider-weighted or arbitrary

scoring.5: Each category has a specific vesting schedule: team has 1+ year cliff with 3+ year vest, investors have 6+ month cliff with 18+ month vest, community has gradual release, liquidity is partially locked; schedules are realistic and aligned with incentives
scoring.3: Vesting schedules exist but some categories are missing or schedules are too short
scoring.1: Mentions vesting but no specific schedules

scoring.5: Each allocation and vesting decision is explained: why this percentage, what incentive does the vesting create, how does it prevent common problems (insider dumping, insufficient liquidity, misaligned incentives)
scoring.3: Some rationale but not for all decisions
scoring.1: Allocations given without explanation

tasks.description: Identify rugpull red flags in a token contract
tasks.input: Review the following token characteristics and identify which are rugpull red flags: (1) Owner can mint unlimited tokens, (2) 5% buy/sell tax with tax going to a marketing wallet, (3) Liquidity locked for 1 year on Team Finance, (4) Owner can set sell tax up to 25%, (5) Contract is verified on BSCScan, (6) Owner can blacklist addresses, (7) No audit, (8) Team allocation is 40% with no vesting contract. Rate the overall risk level.

scoring.5: Correctly identifies all red flags: unlimited mint (#1 - critical), adjustable tax to 25% (#4 - high), blacklist ability (#6 - medium), no audit (#7 - medium), 40% team with no vesting (#8 - critical); correctly identifies positive signals: locked LP (#3 - good), verified contract (#5 - good); notes that fixed 5% tax (#2) is acceptable if transparent
scoring.3: Identifies most red flags but misses 1-2 or misclassifies a positive signal as a red flag
scoring.1: Identifies only the most obvious red flags
scoring.0: Cannot distinguish red flags from normal features

scoring.5: Explains WHY each flag is dangerous: unlimited mint allows supply dilution; 25% tax can make selling impossible (honeypot variant); blacklist can prevent specific addresses from selling; no vesting means team can dump; explains how these could be exploited step-by-step
scoring.3: Explains the general risk but not the specific exploit mechanism
scoring.1: Lists flags as 'bad' without explaining why

scoring.5: Provides a clear overall risk rating (e.g., 'high risk - do not invest') with justification; notes that the combination of unlimited mint + no audit + high team allocation without vesting is especially dangerous; recommends specific actions before considering any interaction
scoring.3: Risk rating provided but lacks the combinatorial analysis
scoring.1: Vague risk statement

tasks.description: Compare launch models for a new token
tasks.input: I'm choosing between three launch models for my token: (A) Fair launch with no presale, immediate DEX listing; (B) Private sale to investors followed by DEX listing; (C) Liquidity Bootstrapping Pool (LBP). Compare the pros and cons of each for a project with limited funding ($20K), a small community (500 Discord members), and a goal of maximum decentralization.

scoring.5: Covers pros and cons for all three models across: cost/funding, decentralization, price discovery fairness, bot resistance, community trust, and required capital; comparison is structured and easy to follow
scoring.3: Covers most aspects but missing some dimensions or one model is underdeveloped
scoring.1: Surface-level comparison with 1-2 points per model

scoring.5: Recommends a specific model based on the user's constraints: $20K budget (eliminates options needing more capital), 500 community size (affects demand), decentralization goal (favors fair launch or LBP); explains why the other models are less suitable for THIS specific situation
scoring.3: Makes a recommendation but loosely tied to the specific constraints
scoring.1: Generic recommendation not tied to user's situation

scoring.5: For the recommended model, provides practical next steps: estimated costs, platforms to use, timeline, initial parameters, risks to prepare for
scoring.3: Some practical notes but incomplete
scoring.1: Theoretical comparison without practical guidance

tasks.description: Design tokenomics for a play-to-earn game with sustainability analysis
tasks.input: Design tokenomics for a blockchain game with a dual-token model (governance token + in-game reward token). The game expects 10,000 daily active players at steady state. Key requirement: the token economy must be sustainable for 3+ years without requiring constant new player inflows (avoid the Axie/StepN death spiral). Include emission schedules, sink mechanisms, and a sustainability analysis.

scoring.5: Clearly separates governance and utility token functions; governance token has fixed supply with value accrual mechanisms; reward token has controlled emission with multiple sinks; interaction between the two tokens is well-defined; each token has a distinct reason to exist
scoring.3: Dual-token structure exists but roles overlap or interaction is unclear
scoring.1: Two tokens named but functionally identical
scoring.0: Single token or no coherent design

scoring.5: Models token flows at steady state: daily emission (player rewards) vs. daily sinks (crafting, trading fees, cosmetics, tournament entry); demonstrates that sink rate can meet or exceed emission rate without growth; includes sensitivity analysis for player count variations; explicitly addresses and avoids the death spiral pattern
scoring.3: Has some sustainability modeling but relies on growth assumptions or sinks are insufficient
scoring.1: Claims sustainability without modeling

scoring.5: Designs 3+ meaningful token sinks: in-game crafting/upgrading (consumptive), tournament entry fees (redistributive), cosmetic marketplace (deflationary burn), governance staking (locking); each sink has an estimated daily absorption rate based on 10K DAU assumption
scoring.3: Has sinks but fewer than 3 or without quantitative estimates
scoring.1: Mentions 'burning' as the only sink

scoring.5: Explicitly analyzes the Axie/StepN failure mode: reward value depends on token price which depends on new buyers; designs circuit breakers (dynamic emission based on token price, revenue-backed rewards, non-token incentives); ensures core gameplay is fun without financial motivation
scoring.3: Acknowledges the death spiral risk but prevention mechanisms are weak
scoring.1: Does not address the death spiral pattern
scoring.0: Design repeats known death spiral patterns

tasks.description: Perform a security audit review of a token contract
tasks.input: Review the following ERC-20 token contract features and produce a security audit summary: (1) Inherits OpenZeppelin ERC20, Ownable; (2) Constructor mints 1B tokens to deployer; (3) Owner can mint additional tokens with no cap; (4) Transfer function includes a 3% tax on buys, 3% on sells, sent to a marketing wallet; (5) Owner can change tax up to 10%; (6) Owner can exclude addresses from tax; (7) Owner can pause all transfers; (8) Uses Solidity 0.8.19; (9) No timelock on any owner function. Rate each feature's risk and provide an overall security score.

scoring.5: Rates each of the 9 features on a risk scale with justification: #1 (low - standard), #2 (medium - centralized initial supply), #3 (critical - unlimited mint), #4 (medium - tax is common but requires transparency), #5 (medium-high - 10% cap is reasonable but still high), #6 (low-medium - necessary for DEX but could be abused), #7 (high - pause without governance is dangerous), #8 (low - good compiler version), #9 (high - no delay for owner actions)
scoring.3: Rates most features but some ratings are incorrect or unjustified
scoring.1: Generic risk labels without per-feature analysis

scoring.5: Describes specific exploit scenarios: owner mints billions of tokens and dumps on DEX (#3); owner sets tax to 10% effectively reducing LP value (#5); owner pauses transfers then removes liquidity (#7); combination attacks (pause + mint + unpause + dump); no timelock means attacks execute instantly (#9)
scoring.3: Identifies 1-2 exploit scenarios but misses combinations or less obvious attacks
scoring.1: Mentions risks without concrete exploit descriptions

scoring.5: For each critical/high risk: recommends specific fix: add cap to mint function, add 48h timelock on all owner functions, make pause governance-controlled or remove it, hard-code maximum tax, consider Ownable2Step; prioritizes remediation by severity
scoring.3: Some recommendations but not comprehensive or not prioritized
scoring.1: Generic 'fix the issues' advice

scoring.5: Provides a clear overall score (e.g., 4/10 or D grade) with methodology: weights critical issues higher than minor ones; notes that the combination of unlimited mint + no timelock + pause makes this high-risk despite using OpenZeppelin base; compares against industry standard security expectations
scoring.3: Score provided but methodology is unclear
scoring.1: Score without justification

tasks.description: Design an anti-bot strategy for a token launch
tasks.input: My token launches on PancakeSwap in 48 hours. I expect significant bot activity based on the marketing hype. Design a comprehensive anti-bot strategy that protects genuine buyers in the first hours of trading while not permanently harming token tradability. Include: on-chain mechanisms, launch timing considerations, LP protection, and monitoring plan. Budget: I can modify the contract before deployment.

scoring.5: Recommends 3+ on-chain mechanisms: max transaction limit (1-2% of supply per tx for first 50 blocks), max wallet limit (2-3% for first 24h), progressive tax (high initial tax decaying to normal over hours), cooldown between transactions, blacklist capability for confirmed bot addresses; explains implementation for each
scoring.3: Recommends 1-2 mechanisms with implementation details
scoring.1: Mentions anti-bot measures without specific mechanisms

scoring.5: Recommends specific launch timing strategy: stealth liquidity addition vs. announced time, block-based vs. time-based trading enablement, multi-step deployment (deploy contract days early, add liquidity at unannounced time), consideration of BSC block times and validator behavior
scoring.3: Some timing considerations but lacks specific strategy
scoring.1: No timing analysis

scoring.5: Covers LP security: lock LP tokens before enabling trading, verify lock is on a reputable platform, calculate initial liquidity amount to withstand bot buying without extreme price impact, plan for LP migration if needed; addresses the risk of bots draining LP through rapid buy-sell cycles
scoring.3: Mentions LP locking but missing depth on LP sizing or migration planning
scoring.1: Brief mention of LP lock only

scoring.5: Defines real-time monitoring for launch: watch first 100 blocks for bot patterns, track unique buyer addresses vs. contract-initiated buys, monitor price impact of early trades, have emergency response plan (pause if needed, blacklist confirmed bots); specific tools recommended for BSC monitoring
scoring.3: Some monitoring suggestions but no response plan or specific tools
scoring.1: Mentions monitoring without specifics

tasks.description: Design a vesting schedule with on-chain enforcement
tasks.input: Design vesting schedules for a token launch with the following stakeholders: (1) Core team: 4 people, 15% of supply; (2) Seed investors: 3 VCs, 10% of supply; (3) Strategic advisors: 5 people, 3% of supply; (4) Community rewards: 40% of supply released over 4 years; (5) Treasury: 20% controlled by a 3/5 multisig. For each, specify cliff, vesting duration, release cadence, and recommend on-chain vesting tools. Model the monthly unlock schedule for 24 months.

scoring.5: Appropriate schedules for each category: team (12-month cliff, 36-month linear vest), seed (6-month cliff, 24-month vest), advisors (6-month cliff, 18-month vest), community (no cliff, 48-month declining emission), treasury (governance-controlled release); each schedule is justified with incentive alignment reasoning
scoring.3: Schedules exist for all categories but some are too short or not justified
scoring.1: Schedules for some categories only

scoring.5: Recommends specific on-chain vesting tools: Sablier for streaming vests, Hedgey for cliff+linear, OpenZeppelin VestingWallet for simple cases, Gnosis Safe for treasury; explains deployment steps for each; notes that off-chain vesting is unacceptable for any category
scoring.3: Mentions on-chain vesting but tool recommendations are generic or incomplete
scoring.1: Suggests vesting without on-chain enforcement

scoring.5: Provides a month-by-month unlock schedule for 24 months showing: new tokens entering circulation from each category, cumulative circulating supply, percentage of total supply circulating; identifies peak unlock months; calculates maximum monthly sell pressure
scoring.3: Has some unlock modeling but not month-by-month or missing some categories
scoring.1: Describes unlock events without a timeline

scoring.5: Estimates sell pressure at each unlock: applies realistic sell rates per category (40% for VCs, 20% for team, 10% for community); identifies months with dangerous sell pressure concentration; recommends adjustments if any month exceeds 2x projected demand
scoring.3: Mentions sell pressure but analysis is incomplete or uses unrealistic rates
scoring.1: No sell pressure analysis

tasks.description: Design a complete token economy for a decentralized AI compute network
tasks.input: Design a comprehensive token economy for a decentralized AI compute marketplace where: GPU providers stake tokens to participate, AI developers pay tokens for compute, node operators earn rewards for uptime and job completion, and governance controls protocol parameters. The token must solve cold-start (bootstrapping supply before demand), avoid inflation death spirals, and scale from 100 to 100,000 GPUs. Include: dual-token analysis, staking mechanics, fee structure, emission schedule, and 3-year sustainability model.

scoring.5: Addresses cold-start with targeted subsidies that decrease as network grows; designs staking with slashing for poor performance; creates a fee structure that is competitive with centralized alternatives; emission tied to network utilization (demand-responsive); dual-token decision is analyzed and justified; includes feedback loops that are stabilizing, not reflexive
scoring.3: Covers most components but cold-start or sustainability is weakly addressed
scoring.1: Basic staking + rewards model without addressing the unique challenges
scoring.0: Generic tokenomics not tailored to the use case

scoring.5: Models the economy at three scales: 100 GPUs (bootstrap), 10K GPUs (growth), 100K GPUs (maturity); shows how staking requirements, fee rates, and emission schedule adapt at each scale; identifies phase transitions and parameter adjustments needed; ensures early participants are not diluted while late participants can still join economically
scoring.3: Models one or two scales but transition mechanisms are missing
scoring.1: Single-scale model that may not work at different sizes

scoring.5: Builds a year-by-year model: Year 1 (subsidized growth, net inflation), Year 2 (transition to fee-based revenue, decreasing subsidies), Year 3 (self-sustaining, protocol revenue covers all rewards); identifies the break-even point; stress-tests against bear market (50% demand drop) and competitor entry scenarios
scoring.3: Has a multi-year outlook but relies on optimistic assumptions without stress testing
scoring.1: Claims sustainability without modeling

scoring.5: Analyzes attack vectors: Sybil attacks on GPU rewards, staking centralization, governance capture by large stakers, fee manipulation, compute fraud (fake jobs); designs countermeasures for each; analyzes Nash equilibria for key participant behaviors; identifies scenarios where rational self-interest leads to network-harmful outcomes
scoring.3: Identifies some attack vectors but game-theoretic analysis is shallow
scoring.1: Mentions security without specific analysis
scoring.0: No security or game theory analysis

tasks.description: Analyze and compare tokenomics of 3 real DeFi protocols
tasks.input: Perform a comparative tokenomics analysis of Uniswap (UNI), Aave (AAVE), and Curve (CRV). For each: analyze supply mechanics, distribution and current circulating vs total supply, value accrual mechanisms (or lack thereof), governance power distribution, whale concentration, and long-term sustainability. Which has the best tokenomics design and why? Which is most at risk of value erosion? Provide evidence-based conclusions.

scoring.5: For each protocol: accurately describes supply mechanics (UNI fixed supply + governance treasury, AAVE fixed supply + safety module, CRV inflationary emission + veCRV locking); analyzes actual distribution vs. planned; calculates current inflation rate and future schedule; identifies value accrual status (UNI fee switch debate, AAVE safety module + buyback, CRV bribes + veCRV)
scoring.3: Covers 2 of 3 protocols in depth or all 3 at surface level
scoring.1: Surface-level descriptions without analytical depth

scoring.5: Compares across consistent dimensions: inflation rate, treasury runway, value accrual efficiency, governance centralization (top-10 voter share), staking yield sustainability, and supply/demand dynamics; uses the same metrics for each protocol enabling direct comparison
scoring.3: Some comparison but dimensions are inconsistent across protocols
scoring.1: Discusses each protocol separately without direct comparison

scoring.5: Rankings and conclusions are supported by specific on-chain data: holder distribution from Etherscan, governance participation rates from Tally/Snapshot, treasury balances, actual vs. projected emission, revenue/token metrics; acknowledges where data is uncertain or where reasonable people could disagree
scoring.3: Conclusions are reasonable but weakly supported by specific data
scoring.1: Opinion-based conclusions without evidence

scoring.5: Identifies the highest-risk tokenomics: CRV's continuous inflation requiring sustained demand growth; UNI's lack of value accrual despite massive revenue; AAVE's safety module risk concentration; for each, quantifies the risk and estimates timeline to potential issues; provides forward-looking indicators to monitor
scoring.3: Identifies risks but without quantification or timeline
scoring.1: Generic risk mentions

tasks.description: Design a secure and innovative token launch for a regulated industry
tasks.input: Design a token launch for a decentralized insurance protocol that must: (1) comply with regulatory requirements (token must not be classified as a security), (2) bootstrap $10M in underwriting capital through staking, (3) incentivize claims assessors and insurance providers, (4) handle the unique challenge that insurance needs large capital reserves but token price volatility can decapitalize the protocol. Include: regulatory-compliant distribution strategy, staking mechanics with capital adequacy modeling, dual governance (token holders + insurance experts), and a mechanism to maintain capital reserves even during bear markets.

scoring.5: Designs distribution to minimize securities classification: strong utility at launch (staking, governance, claims assessment), no promises of profit, broad distribution without investment framing, sufficient decentralization analysis, geographic restrictions for regulated jurisdictions, legal opinion recommendation; distinguishes from a security offering at every design point
scoring.3: Addresses regulatory concerns but some design choices could trigger securities classification
scoring.1: Mentions regulation without designing around it

scoring.5: Designs a mechanism to maintain capital reserves: staking with long lock periods, treasury diversification into stablecoins, reinsurance mechanisms, dynamic premium adjustment based on reserve ratio, circuit breakers that restrict new policies when reserves are low, backstop mechanisms for extreme events; models capital adequacy under -50% token price scenarios
scoring.3: Has capital reserve mechanisms but does not model extreme scenarios or lacks diversity of approaches
scoring.1: Relies solely on token price for capital (the exact problem to solve)

scoring.5: Separates governance into token-weighted voting for protocol parameters and expert-weighted voting for insurance-specific decisions (claims approval, risk assessment, premium setting); designs qualification criteria for expert governance; prevents governance attacks that could drain insurance reserves
scoring.3: Has governance structure but expert governance is not well-separated or protected
scoring.1: Standard single-layer governance without insurance-specific considerations

scoring.5: All components (tokenomics, governance, staking, reserves, regulation) work together as a coherent system; incentives are aligned across all participant types; edge cases and failure modes are addressed; the design addresses all 4 requirements from the prompt with specific mechanisms
scoring.3: Components exist but some work in isolation or create conflicts with each other
scoring.1: Disconnected components that do not form a coherent system
